content.question: Does the Finnish experience of rising SES achievement gaps and informal segregation despite comprehensive schooling represent an inherent late-stage trajectory of comprehensive systems (inevitable under pluralism and choice), or a contingent outcome of policy choices (school-choice expansion, austerity in staffing) that could be reversed by design changes?
content.reduction_path: Longitudinal qualitative case study of Finnish policy decisions on emphasized classes and school-choice policy over 1990–2025; comparison of comprehensive systems with varying school-choice regimes (Germany's Bremen comprehensive vs. selective Länder; comparison of periods before/after choice policy shifts); causal inference on choice-policy timing versus inequality-trend timing.
